question: Explain the primary difference between memory-mapped I/O and port-mapped I/O (isolated I/O).
expectedPoints: Memory-mapped I/O uses the same address space for both memory and I/O devices.
Port-mapped I/O uses a separate, dedicated address space exclusively for I/O devices.
This difference affects CPU instruction design, as memory-mapped I/O can use any memory reference instruction, while port-mapped I/O requires special instructions like IN and OUT.

question: Describe the process of programmed I/O (Polling) for data transfer. What are its main advantages and disadvantages?
expectedPoints: The CPU continuously polls (checks) the status register of the I/O device to see if it is ready for data transfer.
Advantage: It is a very simple method, both in terms of hardware and software implementation.
Disadvantage: It is extremely inefficient, as the CPU is busy-waiting and cannot perform other useful work until the I/O operation is complete, leading to low utilization.

question: Compare and contrast the three primary methods of I/O data transfer: Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, and Direct Memory Access (DMA). Your answer should explain the role of the CPU and the relative efficiency of each method.
expectedPoints: Programmed I/O: CPU actively and continuously checks the device status (polls). CPU is fully occupied during the entire transfer, making it the least efficient.

Interrupt-Driven I/O: The I/O device interrupts the CPU when it is ready. The CPU can perform other tasks between servicing short I/O operations. More efficient than polling but still involves the CPU for each data word transfer.

Direct Memory Access (DMA): A DMA controller manages the entire data transfer directly between the I/O device and main memory. The CPU is only involved at the beginning (to initiate the transfer) and the end (to handle an interrupt signaling completion). This is the most efficient method for large data blocks, as it frees the CPU to execute other tasks during the transfer.
